chunk_summary: This section discusses the recognition, grading, and differential diagnosis of bilateral pitting oedema in severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing its clinical implications and management priorities.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical method for diagnosing bilateral pitting oedema in SAM.
Learn to classify oedema severity and its impact on treatment protocols.
Identify atypical oedema patterns and differentiate them from nutritional oedema.
Recognize the risks associated with oedematous SAM phenotypes, including higher inpatient mortality.
key_facts: Bilateral pitting oedema is confirmed by pressing the dorsum of each foot for 10 seconds; a persistent pit indicates nutritional oedema.
Oedema severity is graded as + (feet only), ++ (feet and lower legs/hands), and +++ (generalized, including face).
Oedematous SAM phenotypes have higher inpatient mortality compared to non-oedematous cases in Indian facility data.
Diuretics should not be used to treat nutritional oedema due to risks of shock or cardiac failure.
Atypical oedema patterns (e.g., unilateral oedema, hypertension, or persistent oedema despite care) warrant evaluation for nephrotic syndrome, chronic liver disease, or heart failure.
reference_quote: Oedema in SAM reflects systemic metabolic and electrolyte derangements (not isolated fluid excess), and sodium loads or diuresis can precipitate shock or cardiac failure. (WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
document_key_topics: Recognition and grading of nutritional oedema in SAM
Clinical implications of oedematous versus non-oedematous SAM
Differential diagnosis of atypical oedema patterns
